stem: An engineer takes 10 measurements of tire pressure using a pressure gauge. The measurements have a mean of 40.2 psi and a standard deviation of 2.1 psi. However, calibration tests reveal that a pressure gauge systematically overestimates the true value by between 3.3% and 7.7%. Estimate the true tire pressure and provide a confidence interval that accounts for both the sampling variability and the uncertain systematic bias.

ground_truth.answer: Raw mean: 40.2 psi. After correcting for the 5.5% bias: ≈ 38.1 psi. Full interval accounting for bias uncertainty (3.3%-7.7%) and sampling variability: [36.0, 40.2]. Point estimate: 38.1 psi.

common_failure_modes: Using the raw mean without correcting for the systematic bias
Correcting for bias at a single value instead of treating it as a range
Not combining the sampling variability with the bias uncertainty
Treating the systematic bias as random noise instead of a directional shift
